text: Vaginal birth after caesarean section: a practical evidence- based approach Mitali Das Rajesh Varma Abstract In the UK, around 10e15% of the obstetric population have experienced prior caesarean delivery. This article provides a practical evidence-based approach to the antenatal and intrapartum management of such women. This review is primarily sourced from data presented in guidelines produced by UK (RCOG, NICE) and USA (ACOG, NIH) professional bodies. Antenatal counselling should incorporate an individualized assessment of the risks and beneﬁts of ERCS (elective repeat caesarean section) and planned VBAC (vaginal birth after caesarean) modes of delivery. Planned VBAC is associated with slightly (0.25%) increased perinatal risk than planned ERCS, although absolute risks are low for both modes of delivery. This information, coupled with high rates of VBAC success (70%) and very low-risk of uterine scar rupture (0.5%), indicates planned VBAC is an appropriate option for the vast majority of multiparous women with previous caesarean delivery and is cost effective for the healthcare provider. Of signiﬁcance, recent guidance from NICE (UK) adds a further dimension to decision making as it has stated that maternal request for caesarean section is permissible in certain circumstances. Keywords antenatal counselling; caesarean section; maternal mortality; perinatal mortality; placenta accreta; placenta praevia; uterine rupture; vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) Prevalence of women with previous caesarean birth The overall caesarean delivery rate in England for 2010e11 was 24.8%; the majority were emergency (14.8%) rather than elective (10.1%) caesarean births. Extrapolating this data, around 10e15% of the UK obstetric population has experienced at least one prior caesarean delivery. In subsequent pregnancies, such women will opt for either planned VBAC (vaginal birth after caesarean) or ERCS (elective repeat caesarean section) modes of delivery; the terms used in this article are deﬁned in Table 1. Since commencement of data collection, there has been continued debate about deﬁning what caesarean section rate is acceptable and achieves maximal maternal and infant health protection without exposing the mother to excess surgical risk. The overall caesarean section rate in England has been stable at 24e25% since 2005. However, there is considerable geographical variation in caesarean section rates (15e32%) which largely arise due to differences in the rates of emergency caesarean section. There is emerging evidence of serious maternal risk that accumulates with multiple (4) caesarean births. There is a generalized consensus that planned VBAC is a clini- cally safe choice for the majority of women with single previous lower transverse caesarean delivery. Such a strategy is also sup- ported by health economic modelling (VBAC is cheaper than ERCS) and would also at least limit any escalation of the caesarean section rate. Nevertheless, there is no overwhelming dominance from clinical safety or economical modelling to suggest a preferred mode of birth, therefore it is recommended that women with previous caesarean birth have the right to choose VBAC or ERCS delivery options in consultation with their healthcare profes- sionals; any additional costs incurred by opting for ERCS are relatively small and justiﬁable in terms of additional beneﬁt. Importantly, this result does underline the justiﬁcation for a publicly funded body like the NHS not to make caesarean section available purely on grounds of maternal request. Nevertheless, recent data suggest a decline in VBAC uptake, which is fuelled by: differences in local Trust VBAC policy and implementation; increased public awareness of risks of VBAC; the accepted relative safety of 2 ERCS deliveries. This trend is likely to be accentuated given women are currently delaying age of childbearing, opting for lower parity, experiencing higher BMI and opting for caesarean delivery (primary or ERCS) on the basis of maternal request. Clinical guidelines and trends Speciﬁc guidelines have been developed to ensure women with previous caesarean delivery can make an informed choice on mode of delivery and that the decision-making process involves individualized risk assessment and is evidence-based. In the UK, health professionals may refer to: RCOG’s birth after caesarean section (2007) and NICE caesarean section (2011) guidelines. Similarly, in the USA, guidelines have been developed by NIH (2010) and ACOG (2010). Data on the risks and beneﬁts of ERCS and VBAC have been largely determined through observational analysis of the eventual modes of delivery: clear discrimination between planned, successful and unsuccessful VBAC is rarely made. At present, there are no published randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing planned VBAC against planned ERCS. Hence, there is no data based on an intention to treat (ITT) analysis where the outcomes are compared in groups that have speciﬁcally opted for planned VBAC or planned ERCS. However, studies produced by the US National Institute of Child Health and Human Development There is no overwhelming dominance from clinical safety or economical modelling to suggest a preferred mode of birth, therefore it is recommended that women with previous caesarean birth have the right to choose VBAC or ERCS delivery options in consultation with their healthcare professionals. Mitali Das MBBS is a Foundation Doctor FY1 at Guy’s and St. Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, London, UK. Conﬂicts of interest: none declared. Rajesh Varma MA (Cantab) PhD MRCOG MBBS (Hons) is a Consultant Obstetri- cian and Gynaecologist at Guy’s and St. Thomas’ NHS Foundation Trust, London, UK. Conﬂicts of interest: none declared. REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 177  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. (NICHD) have overcome many of the shortcomings of previous observational analysis through its analysis of a large multicentre prospective cohort of around 18,000 planned VBACs and 16,000 planned ERCSs with standardized inclusion criteria and outcomes. Management In the vast majority of cases, pregnant women who have previ- ously undergone caesarean delivery are considered low-risk antenatally but high-risk intrapartum. Consequently, antenatal care can be predominantly midwifery-GP led and only interjected with consultant obstetrician-led care if speciﬁc complications arise. Intrapartum care for these women should be at an obste- trician staffed unit, or at a unit where there is immediate access to caesarean section, on-site blood transfusion and maternal/ neonatal resuscitation services. A summary of the schedule of antenatal care and essential management issues are depicted in Tables 2 and 3; for most women, the decision for mode of delivery would be ﬁnalized at 36e37 weeks’ gestation. Placental localization: considering all pregnancies (both scarred and unscarred uteri), approximately 4% are identiﬁed to a have low-lying placenta at midtrimester (18 þ0 to 20þ6 weeks) ultra- sound. Upon repeat scan for placental localization at 36 weeks, only 10e15% of this original low-lying cohort will be diagnosed as placental praevia; this equates to an overall prevalence for placenta praevia of 0.5%. The number of previous caesarean deliveries positively correlates to the risk of placenta praevia and placenta accreta. Women with 1, 2, 3 previous caesarean deliveries experience 1%, 2%, 5% risk of placental praevia and 0.3%, 0.6% and 2.4% risk of placental accreta in subsequent pregnancies, respectively. Major maternal morbidity, placenta accreta and hysterectomy risks are all heightened with an increasing number of prior caesarean deliveries in the presence of placental praevia. Given these associations, it is recommended that women identiﬁed to have low-lying midtrimester placenta who have a history of previous caesarean be re-scanned at 32e34 weeks. Furthermore, women diagnosed with placenta praevia (particu- larly anterior and major praevias) at 32e34 weeks should undergo colour-ﬂow Doppler ultrasound scan as an initial diag- nostic screening test for accreta (morbidly adherent placenta). If accreta is suspected or conﬁrmed, then MRI may be performed to conﬁrm the diagnosis and clarify the degree of invasion. Diagnosing praevia or accreta by 34e36 weeks’ gestation enables adequate time for a multiprofessional care plan to be devised in advance of the scheduled 39þ0 week planned caesarean. In such cases, the woman should be counselled about the increased surgical risks (i.e. haemorrhage, transfusion, organ injury and hysterectomy) and consideration given to performing the delivery: by consultant obstetrician and consultant anaes- thetist team in main theatres; arranging for immediate avail- ability of cell-salvage/blood transfusion/blood products; having on stand-by, interventional radiologist (for potential uterine artery embolization), consultant gynaecologist (if hysterectomy is required), consultant urologist (if bladder penetration is likely) and senior haematologist. Review of the previous caesarean delivery: in relation to the previous caesarean delivery, it is important to identify the indi- cation for caesarean, the uterine incision used and any peri- operative complications. Ideally, there should be access to the woman’s previous obstetric medical record to verify this infor- mation. A previous caesarean delivery that was complicated by Deﬁnitions of terms Planned VBAC Planned VBAC (vaginal birth after caesarean) refers to any woman who has experienced a prior caesarean birth who plans to deliver vaginally rather than by elective repeat caesarean section (ERCS). Successful and unsuccessful planned VBAC A vaginal delivery (spontaneous or assisted) in a woman undergoing planned VBAC indicates a successful VBAC. Delivery by emergency caesarean section during the labour indicates an unsuccessful VBAC. Uterine rupture Disruption of the uterine muscle extending to and involving the uterine serosa, or disruption of the uterine muscle with extension to the bladder or broad ligament. Uterine dehiscence Disruption of the uterine muscle with intact uterine serosa. Perinatal mortality Combined number of stillbirths (antepartum and intrapartum) and neonatal deaths between 20 weeks’ gestation and 28 days of life per 10,000 live births and stillbirths. Perinatal mortality rate will exclude deaths due to fetal malformation, unless otherwise stated. Term delivery-related perinatal death Combined number of intrapartum stillbirths and neonatal deaths (up to 28 days of life) per 10,000 live births and stillbirths at or beyond 37 weeks’ gestation. Delivery-related perinatal mortality rate will exclude deaths due to fetal malformation, unless otherwise stated. Neonatal respiratory morbidity Combined rate of transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN) and respiratory distress syndrome (RDS). Table 1 Planned VBAC is appropriate and may be offered to the vast majority of multiparous women with a singleton pregnancy of cephalic presentation at 37 weeks, who have had a single previous lower segment caesarean delivery, with or without a history of previous vaginal birth. REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 178  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. uterine incision extension, endometritis, or wound infection may increase the likelihood the uterine scar rupture and pelvic adhesions. Factors such as cephalopelvic disproportion or failure to progress in late ﬁrst stage or second stage of labour may recur in a subsequent labour and decrease the likelihood of VBAC success. Medical or obstetric conditions that preclude VBAC: planned VBAC is appropriate and may be offered to the vast majority of multiparous women with a singleton pregnancy of cephalic presentation at 37 weeks, who have had a single previous lower segment caesarean delivery, with or without a history of previous vaginal birth. Cautions and contraindications to VBAC may be considered in four main categories:  Two or more previous caesarean deliveries. Occurrence of this situation has traditionally warranted ERCS, however, studies have shown no major differences between the rates of VBAC success (75% vs. 70%), uterine rupture (0.7% vs. 1.6%) and hysterectomy (0.2% vs. 0.5%) between women with one previous caesarean compared to two previous caesarean deliveries. Hence, planned VBAC is considered safe and appropriate for most women who have had a single prior caesarean delivery and for some women who have had two previous caesar- eans provided they have factors prognostic for successful VBAC (e.g. a history of prior vaginal delivery). This viewpoint is endorsed by both ACOG 2010 and NICE 2011. A meta-analysis of observational data reported in NICE 2011 guidance showed women who had experienced 4 caesarean deliveries were at similar risks of fever, bladder injuries and surgical injury whether they underwent ERCS or VBAC. Furthermore, although the risk of uterine rupture is higher for multiple prior caesarean births, the magnitude of any additional risk is extremely small.  Previous uterine rupture. This is an absolute contraindication.  Type of previous uterine incision. The rate of uterine rupture depends on the type and location of the previous uterine incision. Women with previous classical, inverted T or J uterine incisions are at high risk of uterine rupture (5%) hence planned VBAC is contraindicated in these women. Similarly, inadvertent uterine extension at the time of primary caesarean is associated with an increased risk (6% vs. 0.7%) of uterine rupture when compared with no prior extension, and this factor should be considered at least as a caution, if not contraindication, for planned VBAC. Women who have undergone abdominal myomectomy where the intrauterine cavity had been breached are also at increased risk of uterine rupture (but unable to quantify); there is a consensus opinion that the Schedule of antenatal care 12 weeks Provision of written patient information on delivery options (VBAC and ERCS) early on in the pregnancy. 18e21 weeks Ultrasonographer: midtrimester fetal anomaly scan and placental localization. Reschedule ultrasound 32e34 weeks for women identiﬁed to have low-lying placenta with a history of previous caesarean delivery. 16e28 weeks Consultant midwife-led antenatal counselling appointment for women with uncomplicated singleton pregnancies and single previous lower segment caesarean delivery. Documented counselling of risks and beneﬁts of VBAC vs. ERCS. A review of the previous caesarean delivery, with access to woman’s previous obstetric medical record. Midwifery review for all pregnant women. Undertake routine reviews and completion of 28 weeks screening tests (e.g. FBC, ABO RhD group status, administration of Anti-D). 32e34 weeks Consultant obstetrician-led assessment of women with previous caesarean delivery who are identiﬁed to have a low- lying placenta at 20 weeks and 32e34 weeks obstetric ultrasound. The aim is to provide adequate time for investigation and management of possible placenta accreta. Midwifery review for women with normally sited placenta. Establish women’s wish for planned VBAC or ERCS, following earlier consultant midwife review, and ensure suitability for planned VBAC (i.e. cephalic presentation, no obstetric complications). 36 weeks Consultant obstetrician-led assessment to determine mode of delivery for women who: opted for ERCS; are undecided on mode of delivery, or have complicating obstetric and medical disorders (e.g. two previous caesarean deliveries, multiple pregnancy, macrosomia, IUGR, pre-eclampsia etc). Midwifery review to conﬁrm suitability for planned VBAC (i.e. woman understands all risks/beneﬁts, has normally grown fetus with cephalic presentation). 39 weeks Performing ERCS. If ERCS is required prior to 39 weeks for obstetric or medical indications then prophylactic antenatal corticosteroids should be administered to reduce the risk of neonatal respiratory morbidity (transient tachypnoea of the newborn, respiratory distress syndrome). 41 weeks Consultant obstetrician-led assessment for women who had opted for planned VBAC but have not gone into spontaneous labour. Risks and beneﬁts of various options are discussed and documented. Options include: membrane sweep, prostaglandin or amniotomy induction of labour or ERCS or expectant management. Table 2 REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 179  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. degree of myometrial disruption would warrant caesarean delivery.  Medical or obstetric complications that preclude vaginal delivery. Placenta praevia and cervical ﬁbroid are absolute contraindications. Most authorities would consider breech presentation, ECV for breech presentation and multiple pregnancy as contraindications to VBAC. However, based on limited evidence of safety, ACOG considers VBAC is not contraindicated in such circumstances, although careful individualized risk assessment would still be required. Importantly, for some women in the above groups, who also face severe pregnancy complications (such as miscarriage, intrauterine fetal death, extreme prematurity), the vaginal route for delivery, although risky, may not necessarily be contraindicated. Antenatal counselling Where possible, outcomes from women who give birth at term are used for the purposes of antenatal counselling and throughout this review. Risks and beneﬁts of planned VBAC vs. ERCS at 39 þ0 weeks’ gestation: planned VBAC and ERCS at 39þ0 weeks’ gestation are associated with maternal and fetal risks, and these are summa- rized in Table 4. Women should be made aware that the greatest risk of adverse outcome occurs in an unsuccessful trial of VBAC resulting in emergency caesarean delivery. Nevertheless, successful VBAC has the fewest complications and therefore the chances of VBAC success or failure are an important determinant when choosing the mode of delivery. Planned VBAC adverse maternal outcomes e maternal morbidity is increased in planned VBAC compared to ERCS. The NICHD study showed planned VBAC, compared to ERCS, increased the risk of uterine rupture (0.5% vs. 0.02%), blood transfusion (1.7% vs. 1.0%) and endometritis (2.9% vs. 1.8%). Unsuccessful compared to successful VBAC increased the risk of uterine rupture (2.3% vs. 0.11%), uterine dehiscence (2.1% vs. 0.15%), hysterectomy (0.5% vs. 0.15%), transfusion (3.2% vs. 1.2%) and endometritis (7.7% vs. 1.2%). Planned VBAC adverse perinatal outcomes e this includes: 10 per 10,000 prospective risk of antepartum stillbirth beyond 39 weeks whilst awaiting spontaneous labour; 8 per 10,000 risk of hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy, HIE (approximately half of the increased risk of HIE is related to uterine rupture and is 4.6 per 10,000); 4 per 10,000 risk of term delivery-related peri- natal death (compared to 1 per 10,000 risk with ERCS). Planned ERCS and adverse perinatal outcome e ERCS compared to planned VBAC increased the risks of transient tachypnoea of the newborn (TTN) (5% vs. 2%) and respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) (0.5% vs. <0.05%). ERCS compared to planned VBAC increases the risk of maternal mortality (13 per 100,000 vs. 4 per 100,000). The NICHD study showed there was around a 3-fold increase (0.38% vs. 0.13%; OR 2.90 [95% CI 1.74e4.81]) for one or more serious adverse perinatal outcomes (antepartum stillbirth, term delivery-related perinatal death or HIE) for planned VBAC at term compared to ERCS. Hence, it is estimated that planned VBAC exposes the woman to a very low additional 0.25% risk (or 1 in 400) for experiencing an adverse perinatal outcome compared to opting for ERCS at 39þ0 weeks’ gestation. Upon aggregating multiple study data, the NIH consensus group determined that given a hypothetical group of 100,000 women of any gestational age, planned VBAC compared to ERCS is associated with: lower (4 vs. 13) maternal death, higher (325 vs. 26) uterine rupture and higher (130 vs. 50) perinatal death. Individualized likelihood of VBAC success: studies report success rates of 72e76% for planned VBAC after a single previous caesarean. Several pre-admission and admission based multivariate models have recently been published to predict the individualized likelihood of VBAC success. Although this ought to be intuitively beneﬁcial, such models have not been routinely The potential risks and beneﬁts of VBAC and ERCS should be discussed and individualized; the antenatal counselling process should be documented in the medical records. Clinical management of pregnant women who have had one or more caesarean birth(s) Management of pregnant women who have one or more caesarean birth(s) Any contraindications to VBAC C Placental localization-exclude praevia  accreta. C Review previous record and operative notes. C Medical or obstetric conditions that preclude VBAC. Antenatal counselling and shared decision-making process C Explain risks and beneﬁts of planned VBAC vs. ERCS, including the individualized likelihood of VBAC success. C Elicit maternal preferences for mode of delivery and how they comply with future reproductive priorities. Intrapartum care for VBAC C Delivery setting, monitoring, analgesia. C Recognizing failure to progress and/or uterine rupture. C Caution if induction and/or augmentation are considered necessary. Intrapartum care for ERCS C Preferred gestational timing to conduct caesarean (39þ0 weeks). C Peri-operative management. Management of women in special circumstances C Maternal request for caesarean. C VBAC in the presence of high risk obstetric factors. Table 3 In a hypothetical group of 100,000 women of any gestational age, planned VBAC compared to ERCS is associated with: lower (4 vs. 13) maternal death, higher (325 vs. 26) uterine rupture and higher (130 vs. 50) perinatal death. REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 180  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. applied in the decision-making process and their precise role is yet to be established. Induced labour, no previous vaginal delivery, BMI> 30 and previous caesarean for cephalopelvic disproportion/failure to progress (compared to malpresentation or fetal distress indications) are associated with unsuccessful VBAC. If all these factors are present, successful VBAC is achieved in only 40% of cases. Previous vaginal delivery, particularly previous VBAC, is the single best predictor for successful VBAC and is associated with planned VBAC success rate of 85e90%; prior vaginal delivery is also independently associated with a reduced risk of uterine rupture. Greater maternal height, maternal age <40 years, BMI <30, gestation <40 weeks and infant birth weight <4 kg are associated with an increased likelihood of successful VBAC. In addition, spontaneous onset of labour, vertex position, fetal head engagement or a lower station, and higher admission Bishop score also increases the likelihood of successful VBAC. Elicit maternal preferences and future reproductive priorities: the trade off between risks and beneﬁts for VBAC and ERCS is highly individualized because women value particular health outcomes differently. For example, some women may value their baby’s health above all other outcomes. Such women would opt for ERCS, and expose themselves to operative risk, rather than expose their baby to the extremely low perinatal risk associated with VBAC. Conversely, some women may value their own health and achieving vaginal delivery above all other outcomes. Such women would attempt VBAC and accept the small addi- tional degree of risk they are exposing themselves and their baby to during labour in preference to ERCS. Decision aids and speciﬁc patient information literature may facilitate the decision-making process (DIAMOND trial, 2007). The woman’s preference for mode of delivery, and whether this has altered, should be duly respected and documented. The aim of antenatal counselling is to provide sufﬁcient information to ensure she fully understands her delivery options so that an informed choice is made. As up to 10% of women scheduled for ERCS go into labour before the 39th week, it is good practice to discuss and document a plan for delivery if labour starts prior to the scheduled date. There is no direct evidence to qualify what is a “safe” maximal number of repeat caesarean deliveries. In the NICHD study, Risks and beneﬁts of opting for VBAC vs. ERCS at 39D0 weeks’ gestation Planned VBAC ERCS at 39 D0 weeks Mother beneﬁts 72e76% chance of successful VBAC: if successful, shorter hospital stay & recovery. Increases likelihood of future vaginal birth. Able to plan to known delivery date. Less risk of blood transfusion (1%) & endometritis (1.8%) a. Extremely low-risk of uterine scar rupture (<0.02%). Protects pelvic ﬂoor, reduction of urinary incontinence. Option for sterilization if fertility complete. Mother risks 0.5% risk of uterine scar rupture-if occurs associated with maternal morbidity and fetal morbidity/mortality. 24e28% chance of emergency caesarean. 10e15% chance of instrumental delivery & perineal tear/episiotomy. Higher risk of blood transfusion (1.7%) and endometritis (2.9%)a. 0.1e2% risk of surgical complications. Longer recovery. Future pregnancies: likely to require caesarean delivery, increased risk of placenta praevia/accreta and adhesions with successive caesarean deliveries. Increased risk of maternal death (13 per 100,000 vs. 4 per 100,000) compared with planned VBAC (at any gestation); although absolute risks are extremely low. Infant beneﬁts Reduced (1%) risk of transient respiratory morbidity. Avoids 10 per 10,000 risk of stillbirth beyond 39 þ0 weeks. Virtually 0 (1 per 10,000) risk of delivery-related perinatal death or hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy (HIE). Infant risks 10 per 10,000 prospective risk of antepartum stillbirth beyond 39 þ0 weeks whilst awaiting spontaneous labour b. 8 per 10,000 (0.08%) risk of hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy (HIE). 4 per 10,000 (0.04%) risk of delivery-related perinatal death. Marginally higher (23%) risk of transient respiratory morbidity [6% risk if delivery performed at 38 instead of 39 weeks, but additional risk minimized if antenatal corticosteroids administered]. The estimates of risk for adverse maternal or fetal events in VBAC are based on women receiving continuous electronic monitoring during their labour. a In the NICHD study there was no statistically signiﬁcant difference between planned VBAC and ERCS groups in relation to hysterectomy (23 per 10,000 vs. 30 per 10,000), thromboembolic disease (4 per 10,000 vs. 6 per 10,000). b A retrospective cohort study in Scotland showed that the risk of unexplained stillbirth associated with previous caesarean differed with gestational age; the excess risk was apparent from 34 weeks’ gestation at a hazard ratio of 2.23 (95% CI 1.48e3.36). Table 4 Previous vaginal delivery, particularly previous VBAC, is the single best predictor for successful VBAC and is associated with an approximately 85e90% planned VBAC success rate. REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 181  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. placenta accreta was present in 0.24%, 0.31%, 0.57%, 2.13%, 2.33% and 6.74% of women undergoing their ﬁrst, second, third, fourth, ﬁfth, and sixth or more caesarean births, respectively. Hysterectomy was required in 0.65%, 0.42%, 0.90%, 2.41%, 3.49% and 8.99% of women undergoing their ﬁrst, second, third, fourth, ﬁfth, and sixth or more caesarean births, respectively. Therefore, women seeking multiple future pregnancies should be counselled that opting for ERCS may expose themselves to greater surgical risks for future pregnancies (particularly placenta prae- via, accreta, hysterectomy) and therefore greater consideration ought to be given to selecting VBAC. Intrapartum management of planned VBAC Delivery setting, monitoring, analgesia Planned VBAC should be conducted in a suitably staffed and equipped delivery suite capable of performing immediate emergency operative (vaginal or caesarean) delivery and maternal or neonatal resuscitation. There should be continuous monitoring of the labour to ensure prompt identiﬁcation of any: maternal or fetal compro- mise, labour dystocia, or uterine scar rupture (Table 5). Conse- quently, all women in established VBAC labour should receive:  Supportive one-to-one care  Intravenous access with FBC and blood group and save  Continuous electronic fetal monitoring  Continuous monitoring of maternal symptoms and signs  Regular (no less than four hourly) assessment of their cervicometric progress in labour. Epidural analgesia does not mask the signs and symptoms asso- ciated with uterine rupture, and is not contraindicated in VBAC labour. In fact, epidural analgesia has been associated with improved rates of successful VBAC compared to those women not receiving epidural analgesia. However, epidural analgesia may increase the risk of second stage delay and operative vaginal delivery. Recognizing failure to progress and/or uterine rupture Awareness of the increased risk of uterine rupture in scarred uteri, particularly if there is labour dystocia, implies a more conservative threshold to the maximum upper time limit without cervical progress for spontaneous (such as 4 h) or augmented (such as 2 h) VBAC labour (Table 6). Applying such limits, could prevent a proportion of uterine ruptures amongst women attempting VBAC, however, there is no direct robust evidence to recommend what is ‘safe’ cervicometric progress in VBAC labour. Intervening earlier could also impact on subsequent VBAC success: if labour dystocia was diagnosed between 5 and 9 cm, then subsequent VBAC was successful in around 70% of cases, whereas only 13% had successful VBAC if prior caesarean was performed during the second stage of labour. Uterine rupture in an unscarred uterus is extremely rare at 2 per 10,000 deliveries, and this risk is mainly conﬁned to multiparous women in labour. The original NICHD study reported a 69 per 10,000 rate for uterine rupture in planned VBAC (Table 7). A meta- analysis of various studies, including NICHD data, concluded that the risk of uterine rupture in planned VBAC approximates to 50 per 10,000 and in ERCS the risk is 2 per 10,000 (Table 4). Although a rare outcome, uterine rupture is associated with serious sequelae: perinatal death, fetal hypoxic ischaemic encephalopathy (HIE) and hysterectomy. Around one third (1.4 per 10,000 from 4 per 10,000 overall) of term delivery- related perinatal deaths during VBAC labour were due to uterine rupture and one third were due to intrapartum anoxia unrelated to uterine rupture (e.g. due to abruption, cord prolapse). In the NICHD study, HIE affected 8 per 10,000 planned VBACs, and of these, 60% of cases (7/12) were due to uterine rupture. Meta-analysis has shown hysterectomy was required in 14e33% of uterine rupture cases. Early diagnosis of uterine scar dehiscence or rupture followed by expeditious laparotomy and neonatal resuscitation are essential to reduce associated morbidity and mortality. The presence of any of the features indicated in Table 5 is suggestive of this event. An abnormal CTG is the most consistent ﬁnding in uterine rupture and is present in 55e87% of these events. The diagnosis is ultimately conﬁrmed at emergency caesarean section or postpartum laparotomy. Applying caution if induction and augmentation are considered necessary Although augmentation and induction are not contraindicated in women with prior caesarean delivery, there remains considerable disagreement amongst clinicians on their use. Induction (partic- ularly women with an unfavourable cervix or by prostaglandin method) or augmentation of VBAC labour are associated with a 2e 3-fold increased risk of uterine rupture and around 1.5-fold increased risk of caesarean section compared to spontaneous VBAC labour (Table 7). The decision to induce or augment should be determined following careful obstetric assessment and be Clinical features associated with uterine scar rupture Abnormal CTG Severe abdominal pain, especially if persisting between contractions Acute onset scar tenderness Abnormal vaginal bleeding or haematuria Cessation of previously efﬁcient uterine activity Maternal tachycardia, hypotension or shock Loss of station of the presenting part An abnormal CTG is the most consistent ﬁnding in uterine scar rupture and is present in 55e87% of these events. Table 5 Women undergoing VBAC should receive one-to-one midwifery care, continuous electronic fetal monitoring during labour, and their care conducted in a unit where there is immediate access to CS, on-site blood transfusion services and access to maternal or neonatal resuscitation services. The risk of uterine rupture is 50 per 10,000 for planned VBAC and 2 per 10,000 for ERCS. REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 182  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Consultant-led (Table 6). Women who are contemplating many future pregnancies may be prepared to accept the short-term additional risks associated with induction and/or augmentation in view of avoiding the long term surgical risks associated with multiple repeat caesarean deliveries. Prostaglandin (PG) induction compared to non-PG induction (e.g. amniotomy or intracervical Foley catheter) is associated with a higher uterine rupture risk (87 per 10,000 vs. 29 per 10,000) and a higher risk of perinatal death due to uterine rupture (11.2 per 10,000 vs. 4.5 per 10,000) (Table 7). Hence, due consideration should be given to restricting the dose of total prostaglandin exposure or consider other methods of induction such as intracervical Foley catheter. Planning and conducting ERCS Apart from standard peri-operative measures for conducting ERCS (e.g. i.v. access, FBC/group and save, spinal anaesthesia, operative technique), there are speciﬁc issues that warrant further discussion. Delivery at 39 D0 weeks’ gestation Women considering ERCS should be counselled that delaying delivery by 1 week from 38 to 39 weeks enables around a 5% reduction (6% vs. 1%) in the incidence of respiratory morbidity (particularly reducing the incidence of TTN), but this delay may be associated with a 5 per 10,000 increase in the risk of ante- partum stillbirth. Should there be a need to perform ERCS prior to 39 weeks, then prophylactic betamethasone should be administered at least 48 h prior to delivery. An RCT demon- strated a 50% reduction in respiratory morbidity by adminis- tering prophylactic betamethasone to women having elective caesarean deliveries beyond 37 weeks (steroid versus control; 2.4% versus 5.1%; RR 0.46, 95% CI 0.23e0.93) and this treat- ment effect was still apparent at 39 weeks’ gestation (steroid versus control; 0.6% versus 1.5%). Peri-operative management Timing of antibiotic administration to be before skin incision: this achieves a greater reduction in risk of maternal infection than prophylactic antibiotics administered after skin incision; no detrimental effect on the baby has been demonstrated. Ideally, the chosen antibiotic should protect against endometritis, urinary tract and wound infections; co-amoxiclav is NOT recommended. Thromboprophylaxis: the choice of method of prophylaxis (for example, graduated stockings, hydration, early mobilization, low Management of augmentation in established VBAC labour Clinical management issues 1 The decision for augmentation should follow careful obstetric assessment, maternal counselling and be Consultant-led. 2 Oxytocin augmentation should be titrated such that it should not exceed the maximum rate of contractions of 4 in 10 min. Particular caution is necessary when using high oxytocin augmentation doses as there is a “"dose response" for maximum oxytocin amount and uterine rupture. 3 Careful serial cervical assessments, preferably by the same person, are necessary to show adequate cervicometric progress, thereby allowing augmentation to continue. These intervals should not exceed 4 h. 4 If there was less than 2 cm progress after 4 h of oxytocin then caesarean section should be considered. A more conservative threshold of inadequate progress after 2 h of augmentation may also justify consideration for caesarean section depending on the woman’s individual circumstances. 5 If there was 2 cm or more progress, augmentation could be continued and vaginal examinations performed 4-hourly. All rights reserved. molecular weight heparin) should take into account the risk of thromboembolic disease and follow existing guidelines. Management of women in special circumstances Maternal request for caesarean section The recent NICE caesarean guideline (2011) clariﬁes this contentious issue. A maternal request for caesarean section signiﬁes the woman has requested a caesarean section in the absence of any identiﬁable medical or obstetric indication. Such women require review by the consultant obstetrician, and if deemed beneﬁcial, discussion of their request with the other members of the obstetric team (midwife, anaesthetist). Coun- selling should aim to identify (and document) the: reasons for the maternal request, establish whether the woman is fully aware of the risks and beneﬁts of caesarean compared with vaginal birth and ensure the woman understand these risks and has accurate information. Woman who request caesarean delivery because of fear or extreme anxiety about vaginal childbirth should be referred to specialist perinatal mental health practitioner to help her address her anxiety and provide necessary support. Such women are often classiﬁed as making a maternal request for caesarean. However, tocophobia represents a psychological disorder and caesarean is medically indicated as there is concern that the woman would experience signiﬁcant psychological harm should she undergo vaginal childbirth. Despite a full discussion of the risks/beneﬁts of caesarean and the offer of support (including perinatal mental health support for women who are speciﬁcally anxious about childbirth), a propor- tion of women will express the belief that vaginal birth is still not an acceptable option; in such circumstances, women should be offer a planned caesarean section in accordance to their maternal request. Prior to the NICE caesarean guideline (2011), it had been debated whether women with single previous caesarean delivery and an otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy who opt for ERCS, should be managed as women making a maternal request for caesarean delivery. However, this contentious issue has now been resolved, as both NICE (2011) and ACOG (2010) clearly advocate that the woman is entitled to choose VBAC or ERCS delivery options. Furthermore, in those NHS units that still consider ERCS as maternal request caesarean delivery, then the pathway of full discussion and offer of support (as detailed above and stated by NICE) may be utilized and caesarean delivery be undertaken in accordance with the maternal wishes. VBAC in the presence of high risk factors There is uncertainty in the safety and efﬁcacy of planned VBAC in pregnancies affected by: post-dates, preterm, twin gestation, fetal macrosomia and short inter-delivery interval. Hence, a cautious approach is advised if VBAC is being considered in such circumstances. >41 weeks’ gestation: the RCOG induction of labour guideline recommends induction of labour from 41 weeks as this reduces perinatal mortality without an increase in caesarean section rates. There are no adequate data to recommend whether such an approach is equally valid in women with previous caesarean delivery. The risk of stillbirth at or after 39 weeks is between 1.5 and 2-fold higher in women with previous caesarean delivery compared to women without prior caesarean delivery (absolute risks: 11 per 10,000 vs. 5 per 10,1000). Hence, the reduction in risk of perinatal death that occurs by delivering from 41 weeks is likely to be greater among women with previous caesarean. However, in such women, induction of labour compared to spontaneous labour is associated with increased risks of emergency caesarean section (by 1.5-fold) and uterine scar rupture (by 2e3-fold). A reasonable consensus approach would be for women who planned VBAC to have a consultant-led review at 41 þ0 weeks’ gestation if spontaneous onset of labour had not ensued. This review should assess: her likelihood of successful VBAC (e.g. favourable cervix, previous vaginal birth, absence of any obstetric or fetal complications); the priority attached to achieving vaginal birth (such as her plans for many future pregnancies); her understanding of the increased maternal and perinatal risks if induction is chosen; her preference for membrane sweep, spontaneous VBAC, induced (amniotomy or PG) VBAC or ERCS. In practice, this may mean scheduling a ‘provisional ERCS’ at around 40 þ10 days and converting to induction of labour depending on the clinical review at 40 þ10. Preterm VBAC: the NICHD study showed planned VBAC success rates for preterm and term pregnancies were similar (72.8% vs. 73.3%), however, the rates of uterine rupture (34 per 10,000 vs. 74 per 10,000, respectively) and dehiscence (26 per 10,000 vs. 67 per 10,000, respectively) were signiﬁcantly lower in preterm compared with term VBAC. Perinatal outcomes were similar with preterm VBAC and preterm ERCS. Twin gestation: the NICHD study (n ¼ 186 twins), US retrospective study (n ¼ 535 twins) and a review (seven studies, n ¼ 233 twins) have reported similar successful rates of VBAC in twin pregnancies to that in singleton pregnancies (65e84%). ACOG (2010) no longer considers twin pregnancy as a contraindication to VBAC. Fetal macrosomia: a review of four retrospective studies, and the NICHD study, has reported a signiﬁcantly decreased likelihood of successful trial of VBAC for pregnancies with infants weighing 4000 g or more (55e67%) compared to smaller infants (75e83%). A subgroup analysis of the NICHD study showed that in women with previous caesarean delivery for dystocia, greater birth weight in the subsequent planned VBAC labour relative to the ﬁrst birth weight decreased the likelihood of VBAC success. Short inter-delivery interval: a short inter-delivery interval (below 12 months) from the previous caesarean delivery is associated with a 2e3-fold increased risk of uterine rupture, major maternal morbidity and blood transfusion. Furthermore, independent of the method of delivery in the ﬁrst pregnancy, a short inter-delivery interval increases risks of both placenta praevia and placental abruption. Although this information is useful antenatally, it should also be shared with women post- natally to enable them to plan their preferred spacing intervals for subsequent pregnancies. Conclusion Planned VBAC compared to ERCS increases the risks of maternal morbidity, perinatal morbidity and perinatal mortality, REVIEW OBSTETRICS, GYNAECOLOGY AND REPRODUCTIVE MEDICINE 22:7 184  2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. particularly if VBAC is unsuccessful. The principle risk associ- ated with VBAC labour is uterine rupture. Nevertheless, planned VBAC is considered a safe and acceptable delivery option as the absolute risks for adverse outcomes are extremely low for both modes of delivery. During antenatal counselling, it is important to provide complete information on the risks and beneﬁts of VBAC vs. ERCS and individualize this to her medical circumstances and plans for future fertility. Documentation of this counselling (e.g. stan- dardized VBAC consent form) process and provision of a patient information leaﬂet are strongly recommended. Women may then opt for either planned VBAC or ERCS, and their preference should be respected. The NICHD study showed that the additional attributable risk for experiencing a serious adverse perinatal outcome (ante- partum stillbirth, delivery-related perinatal death or HIE) at term for planned VBAC compared to ERCS at 39þ0 weeks appears to be very low (0.25%). It is likely that this risk is signiﬁcantly reduced, or even eliminated, for women who opt for ERCS at 39þ0 weeks, however, direct evidence to support this is lacking.
